title: Utility & indifference analysis
summary:
## Utility theory

**Utility** is the satisfaction a consumer gains from consuming a good. Economists use it to explain the shape of the demand curve.

- **Total utility** — the overall satisfaction from consuming a given quantity.
- **Marginal utility** — the *extra* utility from consuming **one more unit**.

### The law of diminishing marginal utility
As consumption of a good increases, the **marginal utility of each extra unit eventually falls**. Because consumers only buy an extra unit if the price reflects its (falling) marginal utility, this explains why the **demand curve slopes downward**.

### The equi-marginal principle (consumer equilibrium)
A consumer maximises total utility when the **marginal utility per dollar is equal across all goods**:
> **MUₐ / Pₐ = MU_b / P_b = …**
If one good gives more utility per dollar, the consumer reallocates spending toward it until the ratios equalise. Diminishing marginal utility also underlies the **paradox of value** (water vs diamonds): water has high *total* but low *marginal* utility, so a low price.

## Indifference curve analysis

A more advanced tool using **ordinal** (ranked) rather than cardinal utility.

### Indifference curves
An **indifference curve** shows all combinations of two goods giving the **same total utility** (the consumer is ‘indifferent’ between them). Properties:
- **Downward sloping** — to keep utility constant, more of one good means less of the other.
- **Convex to the origin** — because of a **diminishing marginal rate of substitution (MRS)**: the more you have of a good, the less of the other you'll give up for it.
- **Cannot cross**; **higher curves = higher utility**.

### The budget line
The **budget line** shows all combinations affordable given **income and prices**. Its slope = the **ratio of the two prices**. A rise in income shifts it **outward (parallel)**; a change in one price **pivots** it.

### Consumer equilibrium
The consumer maximises utility where the **budget line is tangent to the highest attainable indifference curve** (MRS = price ratio).

## Income and substitution effects

A price change can be split into two effects:
- **Substitution effect** — the good is now relatively cheaper/dearer, so the consumer switches toward/away from it (always *opposite* to the price change).
- **Income effect** — the price change alters *real income*/purchasing power.
For **normal goods** both reinforce the law of demand. For **inferior goods** the income effect works against it; for a **Giffen good** (a strongly inferior staple) the income effect outweighs the substitution effect, giving an **upward-sloping demand curve** — a rare exception.

## Why it matters

Utility and indifference analysis give the *microfoundations* of the demand curve and let you decompose price effects rigorously — the basis for consumer theory and exceptions like Giffen and Veblen goods.

keyTerms.definition: The overall satisfaction gained from consuming a given quantity of a good.
The additional utility gained from consuming one more unit of a good.
The principle that extra units eventually yield less additional satisfaction.
Utility is maximised when marginal utility per dollar is equal across all goods.
A curve showing combinations of two goods that give equal total utility.
The rate at which a consumer will swap one good for another while staying equally satisfied.
The combinations of two goods a consumer can afford given income and prices.
The change in demand due to a good becoming relatively cheaper or dearer.
The change in demand due to a price change altering real income.
A strongly inferior good whose demand rises as its price rises (income effect dominates).

diagrams: **Total & marginal utility curves:** TU rising at a decreasing rate then possibly falling; MU falling and cutting the x-axis where TU peaks.
**Indifference map + budget line:** convex indifference curves and a straight budget line; mark the tangency point as consumer equilibrium (MRS = price ratio).
**Income & substitution effects:** show a price fall pivoting the budget line, then decompose the move into substitution (along the original curve) and income (to the new curve) effects.
tips: Explain the **downward demand curve via diminishing marginal utility** — a key link between the two ideas.
State the **equi-marginal condition (MU/P equal across goods)** for consumer equilibrium.
For indifference curves, justify the **convex shape with a diminishing MRS** and note curves never cross.
Decompose a price change into **substitution (always opposite to price) and income effects**, then classify the good (normal/inferior/Giffen).
Use the **water–diamond paradox** to distinguish total from marginal utility — a neat evaluation point.
